section.title: Judgment on Nineveh, Good News for Judah
section.sectionRationale: The overture gives way to direct address. God turns to speak first against Nineveh, whose scheming will be brought to nothing, and then tenderly to Judah, whose yoke He will break. The movement climaxes in the herald's beautiful feet on the mountains, the very image Isaiah and Paul use for the gospel.
section.thesis: The plot Nineveh hatches against the LORD will be cut off, and in its place God announces peace to His afflicted people: an end to their bondage and the arrival of a herald of good news.
section.frameNote: Three turns: the doom of those who plot against the LORD; the LORD's word breaking Judah's yoke and Nineveh's name; and the feet of the herald proclaiming peace.

units.label: The plot against the LORD undone
units.note: affliction will not rise a second time

verses.text: Whatever you plot against the LORD, He will bring to an end. Affliction will not rise up a second time.
For they will be entangled as with thorns and consumed like the drink of a drunkard, like stubble that is fully dry.
From you, O Nineveh, comes forth a plotter of evil against the LORD, a counselor of {{belial|wickedness}}.

units.label: The yoke broken, the name cut off
units.note: a word to Judah, a word to Nineveh

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “Though they are allied and numerous, yet they will be cut down and pass away. Though I have {{anah|afflicted}} you, O Judah, I will afflict you no longer.
For I will now break their {{ol|yoke}} from your neck and tear away your shackles.”

The LORD has issued a command concerning you, O Nineveh: “There will be no descendants to carry on your name. I will cut off the carved image and cast idol from the house of your gods; I will prepare your grave, for you are contemptible.”

units.label: The feet of the herald
units.note: good news and peace on the mountains

verses.text: Look to the mountains, the feet of one who brings {{basar|good news}}, who proclaims {{shalom|peace}}! Celebrate your feasts, O Judah; fulfill your vows. For the wicked will never again march through you; they will be utterly cut off.

belial.gloss: Worthlessness, wickedness, ruin; the word later becomes almost a name for the utterly lawless. Nahum brands the Assyrian schemer a counselor of belial, one whose whole purpose is destructive evil. The New Testament picks up the term when Paul asks what accord Christ has with Belial, 2 Corinthians 6:15.

anah.gloss: To afflict, humble, or oppress. God says He has afflicted Judah, owning that even their suffering under Assyria passed through His hand for their discipline. Yet the same verb marks the turn of mercy: He will afflict them no longer.

ol.gloss: A yoke, the heavy wooden bar laid on the neck of an ox or a captive people. It pictures the crushing bondage of Assyrian domination. God promises to break this yoke from Judah's neck, the language of liberation that runs throughout Scripture.

basar.gloss: To bring good news, to herald glad tidings, especially of victory and salvation. It is the Old Testament word behind the idea of gospel. The same picture of beautiful feet on the mountains appears in Isaiah 52:7 and is applied to gospel preachers in Romans 10:15.

shalom.gloss: Peace, wholeness, well-being, the flourishing that comes when everything is set right. It is far richer than the mere absence of war. The herald proclaims shalom to a people long crushed, and its deepest fulfillment is the peace with God secured by Christ.

basic.context: Nineveh, the capital of Assyria, has been plotting against God and crushing His people. In this movement God speaks directly: to the schemers He promises ruin, and to His battered people, Judah, He promises rescue. The high point is a runner arriving on the mountains with good news of peace, because the enemy is finished.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real word to a real people under a real empire. It teaches that plots against God come to nothing, that God disciplines His people yet limits their suffering, and that He announces good news of peace. The herald's feet become one of the Bible's great gospel images.
basic.originalLanguages: The Assyrian adviser is a counselor of belial, sheer wickedness. God says He has afflicted Judah, anah, but will do so no more, and He will break their yoke, ol, the bar of slavery. Then the herald brings good news, basar, the gospel word, and proclaims shalom, peace, wholeness, and rest.
basic.typology: The feet of the herald on the mountains, bringing news that the enemy is defeated, point straight to the gospel. Isaiah uses the same image for the announcement of salvation, and Paul applies it to those who preach Christ. Nineveh's fall becomes a picture of every enemy of God's people finally cut off.
basic.christ: The good news here is that the oppressor is defeated and peace has come. That is fulfilled in Jesus, whose messengers carry the news that sin, death, and Satan are beaten and that there is peace with God. Romans 10:15 quotes this very picture of beautiful feet to describe those who preach the gospel of Christ.
basic.god: We learn that God rules over the schemes of the mightiest empires, that He is personally attentive to His suffering people, and that His discipline is measured, not endless. He is a God who breaks yokes and sends heralds of peace.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 52:7 gives the twin image of beautiful feet bringing good news of peace. Romans 10:15 applies it to gospel preachers. And Jesus promises to break the yoke and give rest to the weary in Matthew 11.
basic.oneStory: The runner on the mountains announcing that the enemy is finished is a preview of the gospel itself. The story of God breaking the yoke of His people and proclaiming peace runs from Judah's relief here to the day every enemy is cut off and the peace of Christ fills the earth.

context.literary: The poem shifts from the sweeping hymn of verses 1-8 into pointed direct address, alternating between Nineveh and Judah. Pronouns turn sharply: a word of doom to the plotters, a word of comfort to God's people, then doom again, before the movement lifts to the joyful cry of verse 15. That final verse also serves as a hinge into the battle scenes of chapters two and three.
context.historical: The plotter of evil and counselor of wickedness likely reflects Assyrian kings such as Sennacherib, whose boasts and threats against Judah and the LORD are recorded in 2 Kings 18-19. Assyria's yoke was literal: tribute, deportation, and the constant threat of siege. To promise that this yoke would be shattered was to promise the unthinkable.
context.canonical: Verse 15 is quoted and echoed across Scripture. Isaiah 52:7 sings of the herald announcing peace and the reign of God, and Paul in Romans 10:15 uses the beautiful feet to describe the preaching of the gospel. Nahum thus feeds directly into the New Testament vocabulary of good news.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure God's afflicted people that the empire's plots against the LORD will fail, that their own discipline has a limit, and that God Himself is sending good news of peace and freedom, so that they celebrate and trust Him rather than despair.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes what God will do to Nineveh and for Judah; it does not prescribe human vengeance. The abiding truths are God's sovereignty over hostile powers, the measured nature of His discipline, and the gift of peace announced by His herald.

debates.question: Who is the plotter of evil, the counselor of wickedness, in verses 9 and 11?

views.label: A specific Assyrian king
views.case: The language of scheming against the LORD fits kings like Sennacherib, whose defiance of God and threats against Jerusalem are recorded in 2 Kings 18-19; the counselor of belial personifies Assyria's leadership.

views.label: Assyria's evil counsel embodied
views.case: Others read it less as one named individual and more as the spirit of Nineveh itself, the collective wickedness that continually devises harm against God and His people.

debates.bearing: Either way the point stands: the mightiest human plotting against God is doomed to fail, and behind the historical enemy Scripture discerns the deeper pattern of evil that Christ finally defeats.

study.originalLanguages: The Assyrian strategist is a counselor of belial, worthlessness or ruin, a word so weighty it later becomes a title for the lawless one. God tells Judah He has afflicted them, anah, taking responsibility for their discipline, but declares the affliction over. He will break their ol, the yoke of slavery laid across the neck. Then the movement climbs to its peak: feet on the mountains that basar, herald good news, and proclaim shalom, the full peace of a people set right and set free. These two words, good news and peace, are the seeds of the New Testament gospel.

typology.type: The yoke broken from Judah's neck
typology.fulfillment: Christ lifting the yoke of sin and giving rest to the weary, Matthew 11:28-30.

typology.type: The feet of the herald bringing good news of peace
typology.fulfillment: The preaching of the gospel of Christ, Romans 10:15, and the announcement of peace with God through Him, Ephesians 2:17.

typology.type: Nineveh's name and idols cut off forever
typology.fulfillment: The final and complete overthrow of every power that sets itself against God, Revelation 19-20.

study.christ: The heart of this movement is a herald racing over the mountains with news that the oppressor is finished and peace has come. The New Testament seizes on that very image: Paul quotes it in Romans 10:15 to describe those who preach the good news of Jesus. Christ is the one who truly breaks the yoke, not merely of Assyria but of sin and death, and who is Himself our peace, Ephesians 2:14. The feasts Judah is told to celebrate find their fulfillment in the feast of the gospel, where the wicked will never march through God's people again.
study.god: The LORD is sovereign over the plotting of empires; whatever they devise against Him He brings to an end. He is intimately involved with His people, owning even their discipline as His and setting a firm limit to it. He is a yoke-breaker and a peace-bringer, who does not leave the oppressed without hope but sends a herald with good news.

crossReferences.note: The LORD answers Sennacherib's plotting and boasting against Him, the very kind of scheming Nahum says will be brought to nothing.
How beautiful are the feet of him who brings good news, who proclaims peace, the twin of Nahum 1:15.
Paul applies the beautiful feet of the herald to those who preach the gospel of Christ.
Jesus lifts the heavy yoke and gives rest, fulfilling the promise to break Judah's yoke.

study.oneStory: The runner on the mountains crying peace because the enemy is defeated is a preview of the gospel. God's pattern of breaking the yoke of His people and announcing good news runs from Judah's relief here, through the cross where the true enemy is beaten, to the day when the wicked will never again march through the city of God and the peace of Christ covers the earth.

quiz.q: What does God say will happen to those who plot against Him, verse 9?

options.text: He will bring their scheming to an end; their affliction will not rise a second time.
options.note: The mightiest plotting against the LORD is doomed to fail.

options.text: He may let their plots run for a time, but He will finally bring them to nothing.
options.note: Partly right: God is patient, yet the verse stresses He definitively ends the plot, not that He forgets.

options.text: He will simply ignore them and do nothing.
options.note: God actively brings the scheme to nothing; He does not stand by.

options.text: He will reward them for their cleverness.
options.note: Their plotting leads to ruin, not reward.

quiz.q: What does God promise Judah in verses 12-13?

options.text: Though He has afflicted them, He will do so no longer and will break their yoke.
options.note: His discipline is real but measured, and He promises liberation.

options.text: That they will never experience any hardship at all.
options.note: He owns that He has afflicted them; the promise is that the affliction now ends.

options.text: That their bondage to Assyria will simply continue.
options.note: The opposite: He will break the yoke from their neck.

options.text: That He has abandoned them for good.
options.note: This is a promise of rescue, not abandonment.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the herald's feet on the mountains in verse 15?

options.text: It is the image of good news and peace, later applied to gospel preaching in Romans 10:15.
options.note: Nahum and Isaiah 52:7 give the picture the New Testament uses for the gospel.

options.text: It is only about a military messenger and has no wider meaning.
options.note: It is a battlefield messenger, but Scripture develops it into the gospel image.

options.text: It is a warning of more war to come for Judah.
options.note: It announces peace and the enemy's defeat, not more war.

options.text: It has nothing to do with the good news of salvation.
options.note: The New Testament directly links this image to the preaching of Christ.

quiz.q: What does the Hebrew word shalom, peace, mean in this passage?

options.text: Wholeness and flourishing when everything is set right, far more than the absence of war.
options.note: Shalom is rich well-being, fulfilled ultimately in peace with God through Christ.

options.text: Merely a temporary ceasefire between armies.
options.note: It includes the end of war but means far more: full well-being.

options.text: A private, inner calm with no outward change.
options.note: Shalom is comprehensive, touching every part of life, not only feelings.

options.text: Nothing more than silence or quiet.
options.note: Shalom is positive flourishing, not mere quiet.

quiz.q: How is the breaking of Judah's yoke fulfilled in Christ?

options.text: Jesus lifts the yoke of sin and death and gives rest to the weary, Matthew 11:28-30.
options.note: Christ is the true yoke-breaker and our peace, Ephesians 2:14.

options.text: Jesus brings real freedom, though the yoke He chiefly breaks is sin, not politics.
options.note: True that He frees, but the deepest bondage He lifts is sin and death.

options.text: The yoke is never really lifted at all.
options.note: Christ genuinely gives rest and freedom to those who come to Him.

options.text: Christ has no connection to this promise.
options.note: The New Testament ties the yoke-breaking and peace directly to Him.

questions: God brings the plots of the mighty to nothing. Where are you tempted to fear the schemes of people or powers as if they could overrule God?
God owns that He had afflicted Judah, yet set a limit to it. How does it change your view of a hard season to know that God's discipline of His people is measured and purposeful?
The herald runs with news that the enemy is defeated and peace has come. Who in your life needs to hear that same good news, and are your feet ready to carry it?
Shalom is far more than the absence of trouble; it is everything set right with God. Where are you settling for a shallow calm when God offers you deep peace with Himself?
Jesus says His yoke is easy and His burden light. What heavy yoke are you still carrying that He is inviting you to hand over to Him?

translationNotes.note: Counselor of wickedness renders a counselor of belial, a word for worthlessness or ruin that later becomes almost a proper name for the lawless one, echoed in 2 Corinthians 6:15.
The Hebrew moves between Nineveh, they, and Judah, you; the sense is that though Assyria is strong and numerous, it will be cut down, while Judah's affliction will end.
This verse is numbered 2:1 in the Hebrew text; its imagery of good news and peace matches Isaiah 52:7 and is quoted in Romans 10:15 of gospel preaching.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Nahum 1:9-15. Poetic line fragments joined into running verse text; where BSB prints an em dash it has been rendered as a comma per house style. Note that Hebrew versification places 1:15 as 2:1.
